sp.speaker: Phil.
sp.l: Here mighty Theseus

sp.speaker: The.
sp.l: Say, what abridgementhaue you for this euening?
What maske, what musicke? how shall we beguile
The lazy tyme,if not with some delight?

sp.speaker: Philost.
sp.l: There is a briefe,how many sports are rife.
Make choyce,ofwhich your highnesse will see first.

sp.speaker: The.
sp.l: The battell with the centaures to be sung,
By an Athenian Eunuche, to the harpe?
Weele none of that.That haue I tolde my loue,
In glory of my kinsman Hercules.
The ryot of the tipsie Bachanals,
Tearing the thracian singer,in their rage?
That is an olde deuice: and it was plaid,
When I from Thebes came last a conquerer.
The thrise three Muses, mourning for the death
Oflearning,late deceast,in beggery?
That is some Satirekeene and criticall,
Notsorting with a nuptiall ceremony.
A tedious briefe Scene of young Pyramus
And his loue Thisby; very tragicall mirth?
sp.p: Merry,and tragicall? Tedious,and briefe? That is hot Ise, And wōdrous strange snow.How shall we find the cōcord Of this discord?

sp.speaker: Philost.
sp.l: A Play there is,my Lord, some ten words long;
Which is as briefe,as I haue knowne a play:
But,by ten words,my Lord it is too long:
Which makes it tedious. For in all the Play,
There is not one word apt, one player fitted.
And tragicall, my noble Lord, it is.For Pyramus,
Therein, doth kill himselfe. Which when I saw
Rehearst,I must confesse, made mine eyes water:
But more merry teares the passion of loud laughter
Neuer shed.

sp.speaker: These.
sp.l: What are they, that doe play it?

sp.speaker: Phil.
sp.l: Hard handed men, that worke in Athens here,
Which neuerlabour'd in their minds till now:
And now haue toyled their vnbreathed memories,
With this same Play,against your nuptiall.

sp.speaker: The.
sp.l: And wee will heare it.

sp.speaker: Phi.
sp.p: No,my noble Lord,it is not for you. I haue heard It ouer, and it is nothing, nothing in the world; Vnlesse you can finde sport in their entents, Extreamely stretcht,and cond with cruell paine, To do you seruice.

sp.speaker: The.
sp.l: I will heare that play.For neuer any thing
Can be amisse,when simplenesse and duety tender it.
Goe bring them in,and take your places, Ladies.

sp.speaker: Ege.
sp.l: Here mighty Theseus

sp.speaker: The.
sp.l: Say, what abridgement haue you for this euening?
What maske, what musicke? how shall we beguile
The lazie tyme,if not with some delight?

sp.speaker: Ege.
sp.l: There is a breefe how many sports are rife:
Make choise of which your Highnesse will see first.

sp.speaker: Lis.
sp.l: The battell with the Centaurs to be sung
By an Athenian Eunuch, to the Harpe.

sp.speaker: The.
sp.l: Wee'l none of that. That haue I told my Loue
In glory of my kingsman Hercules.

sp.speaker: Lis.
sp.l: The riot of the tipsie Bachanals,
Tearing the Thracian singer,in their rage?

sp.speaker: The.
sp.l: That is an old deuice, and it was plaid
When I from Thebes came last a Conqueror.

sp.speaker: Lis.
sp.l: The thrice three Muses,mourning for the death
of learning, late deceast in beggerie.

sp.speaker: The.
sp.l: That is some Satire keene and criticall,
Not sorting with a nuptiall ceremonie.

sp.speaker: Lis.
sp.l: A tedious breefe Scene of young Piramus,
and his loue Thisby; very tragicall mirth.

sp.speaker: The.
sp.p: Merry and tragicall? Tedious, and briefe? That is,hot ice, and woudrous strange snow. How shall wee finde the concord of this discord?

sp.speaker: Egeb.
sp.l: A play there is, my Lord, some ten words long,
Which is as breefe, as I haue knowne a play;
But by ten words, my Lord, it is too long;
Which makes it tedious: For in all the play,
There is not one word apt, one Player fitted,
And tragicall, my noble Lord, it is; for Piramus
Therein doth kill himselfe. Which when I saw
Rehearst, I must confesse, made mine eyes water:
But more merrie teares,the passion of loud laughter
Neuer shed.

sp.speaker: Thes.
sp.l: What are they that do play it?

sp.speaker: Ege.
sp.l: Hard handed men, that worke in Athens heere,
Which neuer labour'd in their mindes till now;
And now haue toyled their vnbreathed memories
With this same play,against your nuptiall.

sp.speaker: The.
sp.l: And we will heare it.

sp.speaker: Phil.
sp.p: No, my noble Lord,it is not for you. I haue heard It ouer, and it is nothing, nothing in the world; Vnlesse you can finde sport their intents, Extreamely strecht,and cond with cruell paine, To doe your seruice.

sp.speaker: Thes.
sp.l: I will heare that play. For neuer any thing
Can be amisse, when simplenesse and duty tender it.
Goe bring them in,and take your places, Ladies.
